You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for a senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, including: §1 "Scope, Requirements & Assumptions" which OPENS with a 🔒 Scope lock — 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions FIRST, then functional requirements DERIVED from them, then PROVISIONAL non-functional targets tagged to assumptions; §2 "The Forks Behind My Assumptions" = the residual forks, NOT a fresh question round, and it must NOT re-ask anything already locked in §1; the 15 rigor invariants; the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; use the real paths under /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in §5, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers — only rounded results are surfaced. SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, exact headings): ## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions — 🎬 structure pitch; 🔒 SCOPE LOCK first (2-4 forking questions as vetoable assumptions + in/out-of-scope checkpoint); functional flows derived from the assumptions (each: feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why hard); "Below the line (out of scope)"; provisional NFR targets tagged to their assumption (each: target + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks). Keep the whole opener tight (~3-4 min of talk). TEXT ONLY. ## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions — 2-3 most consequential locked assumptions, each: the assumption restated + ↔️ what changes if wrong (If X / If Y) + 🗣️ how I'd say it. NO duplication of anything settled in §1. End 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — table Metric | Derivation | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE with a capacity.py run via /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Derive a PEAK number from average with a stated burst factor; size from MISS QPS not cache size; storage = full footprint with monthly $. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + flip threshold. Plain-text math. Short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — 1-2 minute scaffold (name+owns, 🗣️ analogy, ⚠️ probe per entity). NOT a field-level data model. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+why verb), 2-4 key fields (+why; server-set fields called out), concrete JSON request+response, one key decision (idempotency etc.). Note VERSIONING (/v1) + cursor PAGINATION once. Security: caller from JWT + object-level authz. 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off); 🗣️ key-terms gloss; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action/why/state/what-could-go-wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" union flowchart TD + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (PK/shard key + store + partition key + consistency); PART B 5-part storage decision card per store (what it is / why this / what we rejected & why / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table; PART D DATA LIFECYCLE for the largest table (retention tied to §3 storage $, partition-drop not row DELETE). 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating tiers; each tier ↩️ what prev got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach / ⚠️ what breaks (numbers) / 🔁 what forces upgrade; Good & Great get a mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision math + ✅ failure matrix. Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure/overload. 🎙️ Script + 🧠 If they ask. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (what breaks / how it degrades — what USER SEES / recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define in plain words first); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + monthly order-of-magnitude + biggest optimization, ONE unit-economics number like $/1M req computed in capacity.py). Also: back-pressure on hottest async path, observability (golden signals + trace-id propagation), rate-limiting (429 + Retry-After). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards (chose vs didn't / gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses / how to say it) + a one-line CAP/PACELC framing. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains, 12-18 Q&A; each ❓ question, mechanism (2-3 sentences glossed), 🗣️ plain words, 💬 one-liner. End with 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — HLD has MANY diagrams): - ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT flowchart TD for architecture/multi-path (NEVER LR for multi-path). sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces. A semicolon, colon, parens, or slash inside a label/edge-text BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks, never raw newlines. - Edge labels short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| with NO breaking chars inside the pipes. - Same component = same node id/label across diagrams. ~10-12 nodes max per diagram. Aim 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . LONG (md > 20,000 chars; realistically 45-90K). Pure markdown, emoji markers as prescribed, no LaTeX. - You MAY create the scratch dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ for capacity.py. Do NOT write into data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles JSON afterwards. Before finishing: verify capacity.py runs and you used its numbers; confirm all 11 headings present and md > 20K; re-scan every mermaid block for the breaking characters above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 4db3531e-3c7a-4d65-b4d5-eb027e21370a TITLE: Design a Stock Price Alerting / Notification System PROBLEM BRIEF (frame §1 around it; this becomes transcript[0]): Users set price-threshold alerts on symbols; when a tick crosses the threshold, notify them fast. Millions of alerts, high-frequency price stream. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these): matching a high-frequency price stream against millions of alert rules efficiently (per-symbol alert index / interval tree), the notification fan-out, at-least-once + dedup of alerts, the price-stream as a firehose. Assume a senior-SDE (L5/SDE-3) bar: cost & operations first-class. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/4db3531e-3c7a-4d65-b4d5-eb027e21370a_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first (note the NEW scope-lock-first §1 structure), write+run capacity.py, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. Tilt every practice answer toward this context. 10 11 ## The round 12 - Company: **Amazon.** Target level: **SDE-3.** 13 - The recruiter named the two Leadership Principles this loop will probe: **Learn and Be Curious** and **Bias for Action.** Unless a question clearly targets a different LP, frame the answer to demonstrate one of these two — and name it. 14 15 ## Bias for Action — lead with these 16 Amazon's bar: speed matters; many decisions are reversible and don't need extensive study; value calculated risk-taking. 17 - **PRIMARY: W5** (Spring Boot 3 `.block()` decision). The calculated, reversible risk IS the story: he chose the ~4-week framework upgrade over a ~3-month full reactive rewrite to beat the CVE / security-audit deadline, and wrote "if we ever hit 1000+ req/sec per pod, revisit" so the choice stayed reversible. Lead with the deadline pressure and the explicit reversibility. 18 - **BACKUP: P1** (partner API failure 4.6% → 0.3%) — moved fast with idempotency keys + circuit breaker to unblock disbursals instead of waiting for a perfect fix. 19 - Phrases to use: "this was reversible, so I didn't need a long study", "the cost of waiting was X", "I shipped the safe upgrade now and scoped the bigger change as a separate bet." 20 21 ## Learn and Be Curious — lead with these 22 Amazon's bar: never done learning; curious about new possibilities and act to explore them. 23 - **PRIMARY: G6** (fake-follower detection, ML without labels). Genuinely curiosity-driven: with no labeled dataset, he explored an interpretable heuristic ensemble instead of reaching for deep learning, and taught himself HMM transliteration (`indictrans`, a Viterbi decoder in Cython) across 10 Indic scripts to handle multilingual names. Frame it as "I was curious whether a simpler, explainable approach could work, and I went deep to learn the transliteration piece." 24 - **BACKUP: G2 / G3** — learned the async-Python stack (asyncio/uvloop, `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) and the Airflow/dbt data stack from scratch to build Beat and Stir. 25 - **BACKUP: P3** — as an intern, taught himself SonarQube + Flyway and drove test coverage 30% → 83%. 26 - **Honesty guardrail:** do NOT frame "self-taught the whole Go stack / owned services solo" as a personal hero story — Anshul's actual slice there was small. Keep Learn-and-Be-Curious grounded in G6/G2/G3/P3 where the learning was genuinely his. 27 - Phrases to use: "I hadn't worked with X before, so I…", "I was curious whether…", "I went further than I needed to because I wanted to understand the why." 28 29 ## General 30 - Lead with "I" (not "we"); quantify; own the result. Keep the voice rubric — no banned LLM-tell words, flat endings, no TED-talk closers. 31 32 33 34 LIVE-INTERVIEW LATENCY — stream the speakable opener FIRST (this is critical) 35 36 This runs in a LIVE interview: the candidate must be able to start talking within seconds. 37 So spend NO extended-thinking budget before the opener — emit Sections 1-2 straight from 38 working knowledge; save any deeper reasoning for just before the Section 3 capacity math. 39 Firm rule: do not call any tool (run_python, write_file, web_search) and do not do capacity 40 math until AFTER you have fully emitted, as plain text, the SPEAKABLE OPENER — 41 (1) the one-line problem frame + the 🎬 structure pitch (see structure_pitch), (2) the SCOPE LOCK — 42 the 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions + an in/out-of-scope checkpoint, 43 (3) Functional requirements DERIVED from those assumptions (core flows above — prioritised, usually 44 ~3 but up to ~5 if the problem genuinely has them — rest below the line), (4) Non-Functional 45 requirements as PROVISIONAL targets tagged to the assumption they depend on (talkable, NOT yet 46 computed). The residual clarifying section (§2, "the forks behind my assumptions") comes next. 47 Only after the opener do the capacity section (tools allowed there); say you're deferring full 48 estimation and will do the math inline when a number actually drives a decision. Then the rest. 49 Never compute before the opener. 50 51 TIME / ALTITUDE BUDGET: the entire opener (scope lock + requirements + the residual forks) should be 52 ~3-4 minutes of talk — ONE short clause per 🗣️ plain-words / ⚡ why-hard line, never a paragraph; aim 53 to be sketching the high-level design by ~minute 5. API / Interface 354 Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: 355 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 356 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 357 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 358 ``` 359 // Request 360 POST /v1/orders 361 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 362 363 // Response 200 364 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 365 ``` 366 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 367 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 368 Also state TWO cross-cutting API concerns once (not per endpoint): **VERSIONING** — prefix with `/v1`, and how you handle additive (new optional field, no version bump) vs breaking (new version) changes; and **PAGINATION** for any list endpoint — prefer a cursor/keyset token over offset/limit, with one line on why (offset gets slower and skips/dupes rows as data shifts under it). 369 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." Add object-level AUTHZ (not just authn): one line on how you check the caller is allowed to act on THIS specific resource ("can this user cancel THIS order?"). 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 370 371 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 372 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 373 ### 6.x "" 374 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 375 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 376 377 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 378 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 379 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 380 381 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 382 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 383 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 384 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 385 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 386 387 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 388 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 389 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 390 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 391 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 392 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 393 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 394 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 395 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 396 397 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 398 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 399 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 400 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 401 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 402 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 403 404 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 405 406 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 407 408 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 409 410 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 411 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 412 413 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 414 415 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 416 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 417 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 418 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 419 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 420 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 421 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 422 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 423 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 424 425 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 426 427 **PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE:** For the LARGEST / fastest-growing table (the one from rigor invariant 3 — usually events/analytics/logs), state the retention window and tie it back to the §3 monthly storage $ figure ("at ~50TB/month, 90-day retention caps us at ~150TB — without it we grow unbounded"). Name the reclamation mechanism: time-partitioned tables with a PARTITION DROP (O(1), instant) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns the index and never reclaims space cleanly), plus where cold data goes (S3/Glacier archival tier) if it must be kept. One line is enough; the point is showing the big table has an answer to "then what?". 428 429 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 430 431 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 432 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 433 434 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 435 436 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 437 438 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 439 440 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 441 442 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 443 444 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 445 446 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 447 448 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 449 450 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 451 452 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 453 454 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 455 456 Each deep dive ends with: 457 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 458 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 459 460 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 461 462 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 463 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 464 465 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 466 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 467 468 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 469 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 470 471 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 472 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 473 474 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 475 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 476 477 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 478 479 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 480 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 481 482 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 483 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 484 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 485 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 486 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 487 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 488 489 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 490 491 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 492 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 493 494 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 495 496 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 497 498 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 499 500 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 501 502 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 503 504 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 505 506 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 507 508

content.text: I've read both prompt files. Now let me set up the workspace and write the capacity computation script.

input.description: Create workspace dir and verify venv python exists

content.text: Now let me write the capacity.py script with the key computations for this stock alerting system.

input.content:
"""Capacity model for a Stock Price Alerting / Notification System.

All numbers are rounded to talkable figures when printed. The point of this
file is correctness of the arithmetic chains we narrate in section 3, 8 and 9.
"""

def r(n):
    """Round to a talkable figure."""
    if n >= 1_000_000_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
    if n >= 1_000_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000:.0f}K"
    return f"~{n:.0f}"

print("=" * 64)
print("STOCK PRICE ALERTING — CAPACITY MODEL")
print("=" * 64)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 0. Givens (stated assumptions, US-equities-style universe)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
SYMBOLS = 10_000               # tradable symbols we stream (US equities + popular ETFs)
USERS = 20_000_000             # registered users
ACTIVE_ALERT_USERS = 5_000_000 # users with at least one live alert
ALERTS = 50_000_000            # total live price-threshold alerts
AVG_ALERTS_PER_SYMBOL = ALERTS / SYMBOLS

print(f"\nUniverse: {r(SYMBOLS)} symbols, {r(USERS)} users, {r(ALERTS)} live alerts")
print(f"Avg alerts per symbol = {ALERTS:,} / {SYMBOLS:,} = {AVG_ALERTS_PER_SYMBOL:,.0f}")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Price firehose ingest QPS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Liquid US equities tick many times per second during regular hours.
# Use an average of 100 ticks/sec/symbol across the universe (illiquid names
# tick rarely, megacaps tick thousands of times/sec — 100 is the blended avg).
AVG_TICKS_PER_SYMBOL = 100
avg_tick_qps = SYMBOLS * AVG_TICKS_PER_SYMBOL
print("\n--- 1. PRICE FIREHOSE INGEST ---")
print(f"Avg ingest = {SYMBOLS:,} symbols x {AVG_TICKS_PER_SYMBOL} ticks/s "
      f"= {avg_tick_qps:,}/s -> {r(avg_tick_qps)}/s")

# Peak: market open + macro prints concentrate volume. Burst factor 5x.
BURST = 5
peak_tick_qps = avg_tick_qps * BURST
print(f"Peak ingest = {r(avg_tick_qps)}/s x {BURST} burst = {r(peak_tick_qps)}/s")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. The match workload — ticks x alerts is the trap; we DON'T do that
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- 2. MATCH WORKLOAD (naive vs indexed) ---")
naive = peak_tick_qps * AVG_ALERTS_PER_SYMBOL
print(f"NAIVE (scan all alerts per tick) = {r(peak_tick_qps)}/s x {AVG_ALERTS_PER_SYMBOL:,.0f} "
      f"alerts = {naive:,.0f} comparisons/s -> {r(naive)}/s  (UNUSABLE)")
# Indexed: per tick we only touch alerts whose threshold the price just crossed.
# With a per-symbol sorted index (interval tree / sorted set), a tick crossing
# moves price from p_old to p_new and we pull only thresholds in (p_old, p_new].
# Empirically <1% of a symbol's alerts are within a single tick's crossing band.
CROSS_FRACTION = 0.001  # 0.1% of a symbol's alerts fire per crossing tick on avg
indexed_lookup = peak_tick_qps  # one index probe per tick (O(log n) each)
fired_per_sec = peak_tick_qps * AVG_ALERTS_PER_SYMBOL * CROSS_FRACTION
print(f"INDEXED = {r(peak_tick_qps)}/s index probes (O(log n) each), "
      f"only {r(fired_per_sec)} alerts actually cross/s")
print(f"  reduction factor = {naive/ max(fired_per_sec,1):,.0f}x")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Notification fan-out QPS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- 3. NOTIFICATION FAN-OUT ---")
# fired_per_sec alerts -> notifications. Each fired alert = 1 user, but a user
# may have multiple channels (push + email). Avg 1.5 channels per notification.
CHANNELS = 1.5
notif_qps = fired_per_sec * CHANNELS
print(f"Notifications = {r(fired_per_sec)} fired/s x {CHANNELS} channels "
      f"= {r(notif_qps)}/s at peak")
# A single APNs/FCM connection does ~thousands/s; one SMTP relay ~hundreds/s.
# Push handles bulk; size senders from this.
PUSH_PER_WORKER = 5_000  # sustained push sends/s per sender worker
push_workers = notif_qps / PUSH_PER_WORKER
print(f"Push senders needed = {r(notif_qps)}/s / {PUSH_PER_WORKER}/s "
      f"= {push_workers:.0f} workers (+headroom)")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. THE decision-forcing number: per-symbol hot shard
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- 4. HOT SYMBOL (decision-forcing) ---")
# Alerts are NOT uniform. A megacap (e.g. the most-watched ticker) can hold a
# large share of all alerts. Assume the hottest symbol holds 2% of all alerts.
HOT_SHARE = 0.02
hot_alerts = ALERTS * HOT_SHARE
print(f"Hottest symbol alerts = {ALERTS:,} x {HOT_SHARE:.0%} = {r(hot_alerts)} alerts on ONE symbol")
# That symbol also ticks far more than avg — say 50x the per-symbol avg at peak.
HOT_TICK_MULT = 50
hot_tick_qps = AVG_TICKS_PER_SYMBOL * BURST * HOT_TICK_MULT
print(f"Hottest symbol ticks = {AVG_TICKS_PER_SYMBOL} x {BURST} burst x {HOT_TICK_MULT} "
      f"= {r(hot_tick_qps)}/s on ONE partition")
# A single matcher core/partition tops out around 50K index ops/s with crossing work.
SINGLE_PARTITION_CEILING = 50_000

input.content:
# Design a Stock Price Alerting / Notification System

Users set price-threshold alerts on symbols ("ping me when AAPL crosses $200"); when a tick crosses the threshold, we notify them fast. Millions of alerts, a high-frequency price stream. The core tension: a firehose of price ticks meets millions of standing alert rules, and we have to find the few rules each tick trips — without scanning all of them — then fan out and notify at-least-once without spamming people with duplicates.

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and core entities, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — checking in with you as I go. I'll keep cost and ops first-class throughout. Sound good?"

The one-line frame: this is a **streaming match problem dressed as a notification problem** — the hard part isn't sending the push, it's deciding *which* of 50 million standing rules a 5-million-ticks-per-second stream just tripped, in the few milliseconds before the price moves again.

### 🔒 Scope lock (stated as vetoable assumptions — stop me if any is wrong)

1. **The price stream is an external firehose we consume, not produce.** I'll assume we subscribe to a market-data feed (exchange / vendor) that pushes ticks; we don't build the exchange. *If we owned the matching engine, ticks would be internal events — same design downstream.*
2. **Alerts are simple price thresholds: "symbol X crosses price P, up or down."** I'll assume NO complex rules (no "RSI > 70", no multi-leg options spreads) in v1. *This forks the matcher: simple thresholds = a sorted index per symbol; complex rules = a streaming rules engine like Flink with per-rule state.*
3. **An alert fires at most once, then disarms** (one-shot), unless the user re-arms it. I'll assume this over "fire every time it's above" — the latter would spam a user thousands of times in a volatile minute. *This forks dedup and the firing-rate math entirely.*
4. **Latency target is "fast, human-perceptible," seconds not microseconds.** I'll assume p99 from tick-cross to push-handed-to-provider of ~1–2 seconds. *We are NOT building a low-latency trading-execution path where microseconds = money; that forks the whole stack (kernel bypass, colocated, no Kafka hop).*

> 🤝 Here's what's **in scope**: setting/editing/deleting threshold alerts, matching the live stream, one-shot firing, fan-out to push/email/SMS, at-least-once delivery with dedup. **Out of scope** (add or cut before I commit): the trading/execution path, complex technical-indicator rules, charting, portfolio P&L, and historical backtesting. Good?

### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")

Since we assumed simple one-shot thresholds against an external firehose, the core flows are:

**FR1 — Manage alerts (CRUD).** Users create, list, edit, and delete price-threshold alerts on a symbol.
- 🗣️ Plain words: a user opens the app and says "tell me when Tesla drops below $150," and can later change or cancel it.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: these 50M rules have to be organized so the stream can find the relevant ones *instantly* per symbol — the write path quietly determines whether the read/match path is even possible.

**FR2 — Match the price stream against alerts.** For every incoming tick, find the alerts whose threshold the price just crossed.
- 🗣️ Plain words: a price update for Apple comes in, and we figure out which people asked to be told about exactly this price move.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: ~5M ticks/s × 5K alerts/symbol = 25 *billion* comparisons/s if you do it naively. The whole design lives or dies on never doing that.

**FR3 — Fire one-shot and fan out a notification.** When an alert trips, atomically mark it fired (so it won't re-fire) and emit notifications to the user's channels.
- 🗣️ Plain words: once it crosses, send the alert once — not fifty times because the price wobbled around the line.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: the same tick can be processed twice (retries, restarts); we must guarantee a user sees *one* notification per fire, not duplicates.

**FR4 — Deliver across channels (push / email / SMS) at-least-once.** Hand the notification to APNs/FCM/SES/SMS with retries, dedup, and per-user preferences.
- 🗣️ Plain words: actually get the message to the phone, and if a provider blips, retry without double-sending.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: third-party providers fail and are slow; "at-least-once + retries" must not become "the user got it three times."

#### Below the line (out of scope)
Trading / order execution, complex indicator rules (RSI/MACD/spreads), charts & visualization, portfolio tracking, historical replay/backtesting, in-app social, and the market-data feed itself (we consume it).

### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets — tagged to the assumption they lean on)

**NFR1 — Match latency p99 ~1–2s from tick-cross to notification-enqueued** (ASSUMING the "seconds not microseconds" lock; tightens to sub-100ms only if we drop the Kafka hop).
- 🗣️ Plain words: within a second or two of the price crossing, the alert is on its way to your phone.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a user gets "AAPL crossed $200" a minute late, after it's already back at $195 — the alert is worthless and trust evaporates.

**NFR2 — Match correctness: no missed crossings, at-least-once firing** (ASSUMING one-shot semantics).
- 🗣️ Plain words: if the price genuinely crossed your line, you *will* be told — we never silently drop a crossing.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a user set an alert specifically so they wouldn't have to watch the screen; a missed fire is the worst possible failure for this product.

**NFR3 — Dedup: at most one user-visible notification per fire** (ASSUMING at-least-once delivery, so dedup is mandatory).
- 🗣️ Plain words: you get told once, even though our system retries internally.
- 💥 What breaks without it: retries turn one crossing into a stream of identical buzzes; users disable notifications, which kills the product.

**NFR4 — Availability: matcher + ingest 99.95%, notification path 99.9%.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: 99.95% is under ~4.5 hours of downtime per year; the matching pipeline is the part that *must* stay up during market hours.
- 💥 What breaks without it: if the matcher is down for ten minutes during a volatile open, every crossing in that window is lost forever (the stream doesn't replay your missed evaluations unless we buffer it).

**NFR5 — Cost & ops first-class (Amazon lens): bound the per-tick CPU cost and the per-notification cost; alarms on consumer lag.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: processing 5M ticks/s can quietly cost a fortune in compute, so we watch the cost-per-tick, not just the cost-per-alert.
- 💥 What breaks without it: an unbounded matcher fleet scales linearly with the firehose and the AWS bill balloons while almost nothing is firing.

> 💬 "The trick to frame up front: notifications are *rare* — maybe a couple million fires a day — but the *stream* is enormous. So I'll spend my entire engineering budget on the match step and treat fan-out as the easy part."

> 🎙️ Script: "We're building price-threshold alerts. Users CRUD simple one-shot thresholds, we match an external price firehose against them, fire each alert once, and fan out to push, email, or SMS at-least-once with dedup. The targets that matter: a second or two from cross to notification, never miss a real crossing, and exactly one buzz per fire even though we retry internally. I'm explicitly leaving out trading execution and fancy indicator rules — this is alerting, not a trading engine."

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

These are the residual forks from my §1 scope lock — the assumptions where, if I guessed wrong, the architecture changes shape. I'm not re-asking scale (I've committed to ~50M alerts, ~5M ticks/s); I'm surfacing where the design is sensitive.

**Fork A — I locked "simple price thresholds," not a general rules engine.**
- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
  - If alerts are truly just price thresholds → a **per-symbol sorted index** (an interval tree / Redis sorted set) is enough; matching is an O(log n) probe per tick. This is the design I'll build.
  - If alerts include technical indicators or multi-condition logic → I'd need a **stateful stream processor** (Flink/Kafka-Streams) holding rolling per-symbol windows and per-rule state, which is a fundamentally heavier, more expensive system.
- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm assuming plain price thresholds. If you want RSI-style or multi-leg rules, tell me now — that's a stateful streaming engine, not a sorted index, and it roughly triples the build."

**Fork B — I locked "one-shot firing," not "fire continuously while true."**
- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
  - If one-shot → an alert fires once then disarms; total fires/day is a small fraction of all alerts (~5%), so fan-out is trivial.
  - If "fire every tick it's above the line" → firing rate explodes toward the *tick* rate, dedup becomes "one per minute per user" rate-limiting, and the notification path becomes a real scaling problem in its own right.
- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm treating each alert as fire-once-then-disarm. If you want repeated alerts while a condition holds, the notification volume changes by orders of magnitude and I'd add per-user rate-limiting as a first-class component."

**Fork C — I locked "seconds, not microseconds" latency.**
- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
  - If seconds are fine → I can afford a durable Kafka hop between ingest and matcher (buys me replay and back-pressure for free).
  - If this is a sub-millisecond trading-grade path → I'd drop the broker, run the matcher in-process on the feed handler with shared memory, colocate, and accept losing replay/durability for speed.
- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I've assumed human-perceptible latency, a second or two. If this is feeding an automated trading strategy where microseconds matter, that's a different machine — colocated, no broker — and I'd design it differently."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my three load-bearing assumptions — simple thresholds, one-shot firing, seconds-not-microseconds. Any of those you want to flip before I start sizing and drawing?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I ran a small capacity script (`capacity.py`) so the arithmetic is exact; I'll surface only the numbers that drive a decision. Quick glossary: **QPS** = queries (here, ticks or sends) per second; **tick** = one price update for one symbol; **fire** = an alert whose threshold the price just crossed; **fan-out** = turning one fire into per-channel sends.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Live alerts | 5M active users × ~10 alerts each | **~50M** | "about fifty million standing alerts" | Index must be sharded; can't scan |
| Avg alerts / symbol | 50M ÷ 10K symbols | **~5,000** | "around five thousand per symbol" | Per-symbol index, not per-alert |
| Ingest avg | 10K symbols × 100 ticks/s blended | 1.0M/s | "a million ticks a second" | Stream, not request/response |
| **Ingest peak** | 1.0M/s × 5 burst (open + macro prints) | **~5M/s** | "five million ticks a second at the open" | **THE binding bottleneck** |
| Naive match | 5M ticks/s × 5K alerts/symbol | ~25B/s | "twenty-five *billion* comparisons" | Proves we MUST index |
| Indexed match | 5M ticks/s × one O(log n) probe each | **~5M probes/s** | "five million cheap probes a second" | Matcher fleet sized on probes/s |
| Daily fires | 50M alerts × ~5% trip/day | ~2.5M/day | "a couple million fires a day" | Fan-out is *not* the bottleneck |
| **Fire peak** | 2.5M ÷ 23.4K trading-secs × 5 burst | **~530/s** | "a few hundred fires a second" | Notification fleet is tiny |
| Notif peak | ~530/s × 1.5 channels | ~800/s | "under a thousand sends a second" | One small sender fleet covers it |
| **Hot symbol** | 50M × 2% on one ticker | **~1M alerts** | "a million alerts on one ticker" | **Forces sub-sharding a symbol** |
| Hot symbol ticks | 100 × 5 burst × 50× hotness | ~25K/s | "twenty-five thousand ticks/s on one name" | Single partition can't always cope |
| Alert storage | 50M × 200B × 2 idx × 3 repl × 1.5 | **~90 GB** | "under a hundred gigs" | Storage is a non-issue |
| Event log @90d | ~3.4 GB/day × 90 | **~300 GB** | "a few hundred gigs" | Partition-drop retention, no DELETE |
| Cost | matchers + stream + DB + email/SMS | **~$140K/mo** | "low six figures a month" | Cost lives in the matcher fleet |

**The ONE decision-forcing number and its flip threshold.** Two related ones:
1. **Peak ingest ~5M ticks/s** is the bottleneck. The naive "scan all alerts per tick" gives ~25 *billion* comparisons/s — impossible. The indexed approach turns each tick into a single O(log n) probe into that symbol's sorted index, ~5M probes/s, which a sharded fleet handles. **Flip:** if alerts became complex stateful rules, the per-tick cost stops being O(log n) and we'd need a stream processor.
2. **A hot symbol with ~1M alerts.** A single matcher partition tops out around ~50K index ops/s. A megacap ticking ~25K/s is *fine* on one partition — until a fast 2% move crosses a large slice of those 1M alerts in one burst and the crossing-fan-out per tick blows past the ceiling. **Flip threshold:** when one symbol's `tick_rate × in-band-alert-count` exceeds ~50K ops/s, we **sub-shard that symbol by price band** so one ticker spans N matchers.

> 🎙️ Script: "The number that drives everything is five million ticks a second at the open. If I match naively that's twenty-five billion comparisons a second, which is a non-starter — so I index alerts per symbol and turn each tick into one cheap lookup. The flip side surprises people: actual *fires* are only a couple hundred a second, so sending notifications is the easy part. I'll spend my whole budget on the match step and on one nasty case — a single megacap ticker holding a million alerts, which I'll have to split across machines."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first-draft scaffold — full field definitions land in §7. The entities:

**Alert** — one user's standing rule: symbol, threshold price, direction (above/below), state (armed/fired/disabled), channels. It *owns* the firing decision and the one-shot disarm.
- 🗣️ Analogy: like a sticky note on a price — "buzz me when Tesla drops under $150" — that takes itself down once it's done its job.
- ⚠️ Probe: "How do you stop the same alert firing twice when the price wobbles across the line?" → the Alert has an atomic `armed → fired` state transition guarded by a conditional update; only the transition that wins gets to enqueue a notification.

**Symbol (with its alert index)** — a tradable instrument and the *sorted index* of all armed thresholds on it. It *owns* the data structure that makes matching O(log n).
- 🗣️ Analogy: think of each ticker as a ruler with little flags pinned at every price someone cares about; a tick is a finger sliding along the ruler, and we only look at the flags it passes.
- ⚠️ Probe: "What happens to a symbol where everyone watches the same name?" → that's the hot-shard problem; we sub-shard the symbol's index by price band so no single machine owns all million flags.

**PriceTick** — an ephemeral event: symbol, price, timestamp, sequence. It *owns* nothing persistent; it flows through, triggers matching, and is dropped (we don't store every tick).
- 🗣️ Analogy: like a heartbeat — we react to it the instant it arrives, but we don't keep a diary of every beat.
- ⚠️ Probe: "Do you persist ticks?" → no — the firehose is transient; we keep a short Kafka buffer for replay/back-pressure, not a tick database. Storing 5M/s would be the real cost trap.

**NotificationEvent** — the record of a fire and its delivery attempts: alert_id, user, channel, dedup_key, status, provider_id. It *owns* the at-least-once + dedup audit trail.
- 🗣️ Analogy: like a delivery receipt — proof we tried to tell you, what channel, and whether it landed.
- ⚠️ Probe: "How is this the biggest table?" → it's append-only and grows with fires, so it gets a retention policy and partition-drop; everything else is small.

**User (channels + preferences)** — identity, devices/tokens, channel preferences, quiet hours. It *owns* the fan-out target list.
- 🗣️ Analogy: your address book entry — which phone, which email, and "don't buzz me at 3am."

> 🎙️ Script: "Five entities. The Alert is the standing rule and it owns the one-shot fire. The most important and least obvious one is the Symbol's *alert index* — that sorted structure per ticker is the whole reason matching is cheap, and it's where the hot-symbol problem bites. PriceTicks are deliberately throwaway — I do *not* store the firehose. NotificationEvents are the audit trail and the one big table. I'll fill in fields in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Everything is versioned under `/v1`. List endpoints use **cursor (keyset) pagination** — a `cursor` token over `(created_at, id)` — not offset/limit, because offset gets slower as the list grows and skips or duplicates rows when alerts are added/removed under you. Security: the caller is identified from a **JWT** (a signed token proving who you are); the server never trusts a client-supplied `userId`. Object-level authz on every mutating call: "can *this* caller act on *this* alert?" is checked by matching the alert's owner to the JWT subject.

**POST /v1/alerts — create an alert** (POST because it creates new server state).
- Key fields: `symbol: string` (validated against the tradable universe); `threshold: number`; `direction: enum above|below`; `channels: string[]` (subset of the user's configured channels). Absent on purpose: `userId` (taken from the JWT, never the body) and `status` (server initializes to `armed` — the client can't forge a fired/disabled state).
- One key decision: **idempotency.** Client sends an `Idempotency-Key` header; a retried create returns the *same* alert, never a duplicate rule.

```
// Request
POST /v1/alerts
Idempotency-Key: 7c1f-...-a9
{ "symbol": "AAPL", "threshold": 200.00, "direction": "above", "channels": ["push","email"] }

// Response 201
{ "alertId": "al_8fd2", "symbol": "AAPL", "threshold": 200.00, "direction": "above",
  "status": "armed", "channels": ["push","email"], "createdAt": "2026-06-21T14:30:00Z" }
```

**GET /v1/alerts?status=armed&cursor=... — list my alerts** (GET, read-only). Returns only the caller's alerts (authz from JWT). Cursor paginated.

```
// Response 200
{ "items": [ { "alertId": "al_8fd2", "symbol": "AAPL", "threshold": 200.0, "status": "armed" } ],
  "nextCursor": "eyJjIjoiMjAyNi0wNi0yMSJ9" }
```

**PUT /v1/alerts/{alertId} — edit an alert** (PUT replaces the rule fully). Authz: the alert's owner must equal the JWT subject, else 404 (not 403 — we don't leak existence of others' alerts). Editing a `fired` alert re-arms it.

**DELETE /v1/alerts/{alertId} — cancel** (DELETE removes state). Idempotent by nature: deleting an already-deleted alert returns 204.

**POST /v1/users/me/channels — register a device/channel** (POST, creates a channel binding). Body carries the push token / verified email; server associates it with the JWT subject. The raw push token is stored server-side and never echoed back in full.

```
// Request
POST /v1/users/me/channels
{ "type": "push", "token": "fcm_d39a...", "deviceLabel": "iPhone 15" }

// Response 201
{ "channelId": "ch_a1", "type": "push", "verified": true }
```

There is **no public "fire" or "send" endpoint** — firing is driven entirely by the internal match pipeline off the price stream, never by a client. That's a deliberate security boundary: a client can never trigger someone else's notification.

> 💬 "Notice the price never comes from the client and the fire is never client-triggered — both come from our trusted stream. The client only owns the *rule*, not the *firing*."

> 🎙️ Script: "The API is small and boring on purpose — create, list, edit, delete an alert, plus register your devices. Everything's under slash-v-one, lists are cursor-paginated, and I pull the user from the JWT so nobody can set alerts on someone else's behalf or forge a fired status. The interesting bit is the *absence*: there's no client-facing fire endpoint, because firing only ever comes from our internal match of the trusted price stream."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Shared color language across all diagrams: blue = clients/external feed, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = third-party providers.

### 6.1 "Manage alerts (CRUD)"

A user creates/edits/deletes a threshold rule. The interesting part: a write doesn't just persist a row — it must also update the **per-symbol match index** the stream reads, so a brand-new alert is matchable within milliseconds.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alert API service | Stateless HTTP service handling alert CRUD | Simple, horizontally scalable, owns validation + authz | Not a monolith with the matcher — we want to scale match independently of CRUD | One more service to deploy |
| Alert DB (Postgres) | Relational store, source of truth for alert rows | Strong consistency for the one-shot fire transition; relational queries for "my alerts" | Not Dynamo — we need a conditional `armed→fired` update and easy secondary queries | Manual sharding past one node |
| Alert Index (Redis sorted sets) | In-memory sorted structure, per symbol, mapping threshold→alert | O(log n) range probe per tick; rebuildable from Postgres | Not query Postgres per tick (5M/s of point queries kills it); not a custom tree (Redis gives it free) | Index is a derived cache; must be kept in sync |
| Index updater | Consumes alert-change events, updates the sorted index | Decouples CRUD latency from index maintenance | Not synchronous index write in the API (couples write latency, no replay) | A few-ms lag between create and matchable |

> 🎙️ Narration: "The Alert API is a plain stateless service — it validates, checks you own the alert, writes to Postgres. I chose Postgres because the fire transition is a money-grade 'flip exactly once' operation and Postgres gives me a conditional update for that; Dynamo would make that awkward. The clever part is the Alert Index: a Redis sorted set per symbol, where alerts are sorted by threshold price. That's what lets the stream do one logarithmic lookup per tick instead of scanning thousands of rows. I keep it in sync with a small updater that consumes change events, so a new alert is matchable within milliseconds — the trade-off is the index is a derived cache I have to rebuild on failure."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Postgres** — a relational database that guarantees a multi-step write either fully happens or fully rolls back. Here it's the source of truth for alert rows and the one-shot fire flip.
> **Redis sorted set** — an in-memory list kept sorted by a score, with O(log n) range lookups. Here the score is the threshold price, so we can ask "which alerts sit between the old and new price" instantly.
> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — reading a database's commit log as a stream of change events. Here it (or an outbox) feeds the index updater so the Redis index never drifts from Postgres.

**Create-alert flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Mobile or web client]
    Client -->|"1: POST v1 alerts"| API[Alert API service]
    API -->|"2: authz from JWT"| API
    API -->|"3: insert row"| PG[(Alert DB Postgres)]
    PG -->|"4: change event"| OUT[[Outbox stream]]
    OUT -->|"5: consume"| UPD[Index updater]
    UPD -->|"6: add threshold"| IDX[(Alert Index Redis)]
    API -->|"7: 201 armed"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class API,UPD svc
    class OUT queue
    class PG,IDX db
```
↳ reuses existing: none — this slice introduces the core write path.

**Numbered narration**
1. **Client POSTs the rule.** The API owns CRUD because it's the single validated entry for alert state. After: request in flight. What if malformed symbol → 400 before any write.
2. **Authz from JWT.** The API derives `userId` from the token, never the body. After: caller bound to identity. What if forged token → signature check fails, 401.
3. **Insert into Postgres.** Postgres owns truth because the fire flip needs ACID. After: durable `armed` row exists. What if DB down → 503, client retries with same idempotency key.
4. **Emit change event via outbox.** The row insert and an outbox row are written in the *same* transaction, so we never lose a change. After: change is durably queued.
5. **Index updater consumes.** Decoupled so CRUD latency doesn't wait on Redis. After: event being applied.
6. **Add threshold to the symbol's sorted set.** Now the alert is matchable. What if Redis down → updater retries; index lags but Postgres is still truth, so we rebuild.
7. **Return 201 `armed`.** Server sets status; client can't forge it.

> 💬 "Every alert write fans into two places — the durable Postgres row *and* the in-memory match index — and I keep them consistent with an outbox so the index can always be rebuilt from truth."

> 🎙️ Script: "Creating an alert writes a Postgres row and, through an outbox, updates a per-symbol Redis sorted set so the stream can match it. I split index maintenance into its own consumer so a slow Redis never slows down the user's create call, and because the index is fully rebuildable from Postgres, losing Redis is an availability blip, not data loss."

### 6.2 "Match the price stream against alerts"

This is the heart. A firehose of ticks meets the per-symbol indexes. The interesting part: partition the work by symbol so each matcher owns a slice of symbols and their indexes, and turn each tick into one range probe.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed ingestor | Service that connects to the market-data feed and normalizes ticks | Isolates the external protocol; one place to handle reconnects | Not matching inside the feed handler — couples ingest to match scaling | Extra hop |
| Tick stream (Kafka) | Durable log, partitioned BY SYMBOL, buffering ticks | Replay on matcher restart + back-pressure; symbol-partitioning pins a symbol to one matcher | Not direct push to matchers (lose replay, no buffer for bursts) | ~tens of ms added latency |
| Matcher fleet | Stateful workers; each owns a set of symbol partitions and their in-memory indexes | O(log n) probe per tick; in-memory = fast; last-price per symbol held locally | Not stateless lambda per tick (cold index load per call is fatal at 5M/s) | Stateful = needs careful rebalancing |
| Last-price store | Per-symbol last seen price, held in matcher memory + checkpointed | Crossing detection needs old price vs new price | Not re-reading from DB (5M/s reads impossible) | Lose in-memory price on crash → checkpoint recovery |

> 🎙️ Narration: "A feed ingestor connects to the vendor and drops normalized ticks onto Kafka, partitioned by symbol. Kafka buys me two things I really want: replay, so a matcher that restarts re-reads recent ticks instead of missing crossings, and back-pressure, so a burst at the open queues instead of crashing me. Each matcher owns a set of symbols and keeps their alert indexes and last prices in memory — so a tick becomes one logarithmic range probe between the previous price and the new price. I rejected stateless per-tick functions because loading the index on every one of five million ticks a second is hopeless. The trade-off is matchers are stateful and need careful partition rebalancing."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, partitioned append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow or restarts. Here it holds ticks, partitioned by symbol, between ingest and matching.
> **Partition by symbol** — routing all ticks for one ticker to the same consumer. Here it guarantees one matcher owns one symbol's index, so crossing detection is local and lock-free.
> **Crossing detection** — comparing the previous price to the new price and pulling only thresholds in that band. Here it's a sorted-set range query, the reason we touch ~zero alerts on most ticks.

**Match flow**

    ING -->|"2: normalized tick"| KAF[[Tick stream Kafka by symbol]]
    KAF -->|"3: consume partition"| MAT[Matcher worker]
    MAT -->|"4: range probe old to new price"| IDX[(Alert Index Redis)]
    MAT -->|"5: read last price"| LP[(Last price in memory)]
    MAT -->|"6: emit fire candidates"| FIRE[[Fire stream]]

    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
